title: New End Ileostomy in a 26-Year-Old Woman After Total Proctocolectomy for Medically Refractory Ulcerative Colitis: Postoperative Stoma Assessment (Beefy-Red/Budded vs Dusky/Necrotic), High-Volume Liquid Effluent with Dehydration + Electrolyte-Loss Risk, Pouching System + Peristomal Skin Protection, Dietary Teaching + Food-Blockage Prevention, Medication-Absorption Caveats, and Body-Image/Psychosocial Adjustment Education for Lifelong Self-Management
subtitle: ILEOSTOMY = a surgically created stoma in which the end (or loop) of the ILEUM is brought through the abdominal wall to divert fecal effluent into an external pouch; created here after TOTAL PROCTOCOLECTOMY (removal of colon + rectum) for medically refractory ULCERATIVE COLITIS (UC) — a chronic inflammatory bowel disease that, unlike Crohn disease, is limited to the colon/rectum, so removing the colon and rectum is CURATIVE for the intestinal disease (an end ileostomy is permanent; some patients instead have a staged ileal pouch-anal anastomosis / J-pouch). KEY ANATOMIC/PHYSIOLOGIC POINT — because the COLON (which reabsorbs water + sodium and forms stool) is gone, ILEOSTOMY EFFLUENT is LIQUID-to-PASTE, continuous (no voluntary control — there is no sphincter), enzyme-rich, and HIGH IN VOLUME + SODIUM + WATER; this drives the two dominant nursing priorities: (1) PERISTOMAL SKIN PROTECTION (the proteolytic small-bowel effluent rapidly excoriates skin) and (2) FLUID + ELECTROLYTE balance (DEHYDRATION + sodium/potassium loss risk). Contrast with a COLOSTOMY (stoma from the colon): more formed/solid stool, lower volume, less enzymatic, and the more distal the colostomy the more formed the output. STOMA ASSESSMENT (critical, especially early post-op): a HEALTHY stoma is BEEFY-RED to PINK, MOIST, and slightly raised/'budded' above skin level (a budded stoma directs effluent into the pouch + protects skin); the stoma itself has no sensory nerve endings (painless to touch) and may bleed slightly when cleaned (very vascular — minor oozing is normal); mild EDEMA is expected for the first weeks and shrinks over ~6-8 weeks (re-measure for pouch sizing as it shrinks). ABNORMAL/EMERGENT stoma findings = DUSKY, PURPLE, BROWN, or BLACK color (ISCHEMIA/NECROSIS → notify surgeon immediately), or a pale/dark dry stoma; other complications = RETRACTION (stoma sinks below skin → leakage/skin breakdown), PROLAPSE (telescoping out), parastomal HERNIA, STENOSIS (narrowing → obstruction), MUCOCUTANEOUS SEPARATION, and peristomal skin breakdown (the most common problem). OUTPUT EXPECTATIONS + COMPLICATIONS: in the immediate post-op period the small bowel adapts over weeks; expected mature ileostomy output is roughly 500-1000 mL/day (variable). HIGH-OUTPUT ILEOSTOMY (>1000-1200+ mL/day, or watery output) → risk of DEHYDRATION + HYPONATREMIA + HYPOKALEMIA + acute kidney injury + metabolic disturbance → manage with oral rehydration solutions, monitor I&O + weight + electrolytes + signs of dehydration (thirst, dark/scant urine, dizziness, tachycardia, orthostasis), antidiarrheals/antimotility (loperamide) + bulking + dietary measures per surgeon; conversely a SUDDEN STOP in output WITH cramping/distension/nausea/vomiting suggests a FOOD BLOCKAGE/OBSTRUCTION. POUCHING SYSTEM + SKIN: use a properly fitted skin barrier (wafer) cut/molded to the stoma size (with a small ~1-2 mm clearance — too large exposes skin to effluent, too tight constricts the stoma); one-piece vs two-piece systems; CONVEX barriers + a belt for retracted/flush stomas; empty the pouch when ~1/3 to 1/2 full (prevents weight/leakage); change the wafer routinely (e.g., every 3-7 days) + immediately if leaking/itching/burning (a sign of effluent on skin); protect peristomal skin (barrier rings/paste/powder for denuded skin; treat candidal rash); the stoma is measured at each change while it shrinks. DIETARY TEACHING: chew food THOROUGHLY + eat slowly; introduce foods gradually; INITIALLY LOW-FIBER (low-residue) post-op; foods that can cause MECHANICAL BLOCKAGE (eat sparingly/with caution + lots of fluid) = high-fiber/fibrous or hard-to-digest foods such as popcorn, nuts, seeds, raw/stringy vegetables (celery, coleslaw), corn, mushrooms, dried fruit, skins/peels, coconut; ODOR/GAS-producing foods (eggs, fish, onions, garlic, cruciferous vegetables, beans, carbonated drinks) — manage per preference; foods that THICKEN output (applesauce, bananas, rice, pasta, potatoes, peanut butter, marshmallows, tapioca) help loose output; ample FLUID + SALT to replace losses; recognize/relieve a blockage (clear-liquids/stop solids + warm bath + knee-chest position + gentle abdominal massage + report no output with pain/vomiting). MEDICATION CAVEAT — ENTERIC-COATED + EXTENDED/SUSTAINED-RELEASE oral medications may pass through and appear undissolved in the pouch (incomplete absorption owing to shortened transit/absence of colon) → use immediate-release/liquid/crushable forms when possible + consult pharmacy; loperamide for output control. PSYCHOSOCIAL/BODY IMAGE — a new ostomy is a major adjustment (altered body image, fear of leakage/odor, sexuality, dating, return to work/sport, clothing) → empathetic support, involve the WOC (wound-ostomy-continence) nurse, peer support (United Ostomy Associations of America), reassure that a well-managed ostomy allows a full active life; a stoma site is ideally MARKED PREOPERATIVELY by the WOC nurse (away from belt line/scars/folds/bony prominences, on a flat visible surface the patient can see/reach). DISCHARGE READINESS = the patient (and a support person) can empty + change the appliance, recognize a healthy vs abnormal stoma, recognize dehydration + blockage, and knows follow-up + resources

patient.name: A. Delgado
patient.code: Full code; decisional adult; provided informed consent for surgery; goals = recovery from surgery + independence in ileostomy self-care + return to work/active life + emotional adjustment to the stoma
patient.admitDate: Post-operative day 1 from a TOTAL PROCTOCOLECTOMY WITH END ILEOSTOMY performed for an 8-year history of ULCERATIVE COLITIS that became medically refractory (steroid-dependent, failed mesalamine + azathioprine + biologic therapy, with worsening bloody diarrhea, anemia, weight loss, and a recent severe flare with dysplasia on surveillance biopsy). The stoma site was marked preoperatively by the WOC nurse. She is now learning to care for a brand-new end ileostomy and beginning lifelong self-management education; she is motivated but anxious about managing the stoma, leakage, and how it will affect her life.
patient.room: Surgical unit private room; WOC (wound-ostomy-continence) nurse + colorectal surgery + dietitian + pharmacy + social work + ostomy peer-support resources engaged; supportive partner involved in teaching

vitals.temp: 37.2°C; alert + oriented + comfortable on scheduled multimodal analgesia; abdomen soft, appropriately tender at incision, no distension; STOMA in right lower quadrant = BEEFY-RED, moist, budded ~1.5 cm, mild expected post-op edema; small amount of bloody-tinged mucus + scant effluent in the clear post-op pouch (clear pouch used early to allow stoma visualization); peristomal skin intact; first ambulation completed; IV fluids running; voiding adequately

37.1°C; bowel function returning — ileostomy now putting out LIQUID effluent; output last 24 hr ~600 mL; stoma beefy-red + budded + edema decreasing; peristomal skin intact with well-fitted barrier; tolerating clear → low-residue diet advancement; IV fluids continued while oral intake establishes; I&O + daily weight + electrolytes monitored; began ostomy self-care teaching (emptying the pouch) with WOC nurse

37.0°C; ileostomy output increased to ~1300 mL over 24 hr (HIGH-OUTPUT) with watery effluent; mild thirst + slightly dark urine + mild orthostasis = early dehydration signs; electrolytes show Na 134 (low-normal) + K 3.4 (low); interventions — oral rehydration solution started, IV fluid + electrolyte repletion, loperamide added per surgeon, output-thickening dietary measures taught, strict I&O + daily weight continued; teaching on dehydration recognition reinforced; peristomal skin remains intact

36.9°C; with oral rehydration + loperamide + dietary thickening, output decreased to ~900 mL/24 hr with thicker paste-like effluent; electrolytes corrected (Na 138, K 4.1); orthostasis resolved + urine clearing; stoma beefy-red + edema nearly resolved + remeasured for proper pouch sizing; patient successfully emptied her own pouch + observed a wafer change; reviewing dietary + medication teaching; mood anxious but engaging

36.8°C; stable; output ~700 mL/24 hr paste consistency; stoma healthy + peristomal skin intact; patient performed a full pouch/wafer CHANGE with WOC-nurse coaching (measured stoma, cut barrier with small clearance, applied without leakage) + partner also taught; verbalized blockage + dehydration warning signs + dietary list + medication caveats; met with ostomy peer-support volunteer; body-image concerns being addressed; discharge teaching nearly complete

36.8°C; medically ready + tolerating regular low-residue diet + stable controlled output (~600-700 mL/24 hr) + stable electrolytes + healthy stoma + intact skin; demonstrated independent pouch empty + wafer change with teach-back; recognizes healthy vs abnormal stoma, dehydration, and blockage; knows diet + fluid/salt + medication caveats + when to call; outpatient WOC + surgeon follow-up + ostomy supplies + peer-support resources arranged; emotional adjustment improving with support; discharged home

labs.v: 26 y/o woman A. Delgado, post-operative day 1 from a TOTAL PROCTOCOLECTOMY WITH END ILEOSTOMY. INDICATION: an 8-year history of ULCERATIVE COLITIS (UC) — a chronic inflammatory bowel disease confined to the colon and rectum (continuous mucosal inflammation extending proximally from the rectum; contrast with Crohn disease, which is transmural and can involve any part of the GI tract 'mouth to anus' with skip lesions) — that became MEDICALLY REFRACTORY: steroid-dependent with repeated flares, failed mesalamine (5-ASA) + azathioprine + a biologic (anti-TNF) agent, with worsening bloody diarrhea, anemia, fatigue, weight loss, and a recent severe flare; surveillance colonoscopy biopsy showed DYSPLASIA (increasing colorectal-cancer risk in long-standing UC). Because UC is limited to the colon/rectum, REMOVING THE COLON AND RECTUM (proctocolectomy) is CURATIVE for the intestinal disease and eliminates the cancer risk; she was counseled on options (permanent end ileostomy vs staged ileal pouch-anal anastomosis/J-pouch) and chose a permanent END ILEOSTOMY. PREOPERATIVE PREPARATION (best practice): the stoma site was MARKED by the WOC (wound-ostomy-continence) nurse preoperatively — on a flat, visible surface within the rectus muscle, away from the belt line, scars, skin folds, and bony prominences, where the patient can see and reach it (proper siting prevents leakage + appliance problems); preoperative ostomy education + psychological preparation begun. PMH: ulcerative colitis; iron-deficiency anemia; otherwise healthy. MEDICATIONS preop included corticosteroids (will require perioperative stress-dose management + taper) + the failed UC agents. SOCIAL: works as a graphic designer; lives with a supportive partner (engaged in teaching); active (yoga, hiking) and anxious about how the ostomy will affect work, exercise, intimacy, and clothing; no tobacco; occasional alcohol; good insurance; strong support system. POST-OP COURSE: stable, comfortable on multimodal analgesia, early ambulation, and she is now beginning the central work of this admission — learning lifelong ILEOSTOMY SELF-MANAGEMENT with the WOC nurse + team. She is motivated but anxious about leakage, odor, and body image.
labs.r: New permanent END ILEOSTOMY after curative total proctocolectomy for medically refractory ulcerative colitis with dysplasia. Preoperative WOC stoma marking + education set her up for success. The admission's central goal is safe recovery + lifelong ileostomy self-management teaching + psychosocial adjustment. RN role: post-op care + comprehensive ostomy education + emotional support.

labs.v: STOMA ASSESSMENT is a core nursing skill, especially in the early post-operative period when ischemia/necrosis must be detected promptly. A HEALTHY STOMA is: BEEFY-RED to PINK (well perfused), MOIST, and slightly raised/'BUDDED' (protruding ~1-2.5 cm above the skin — a budded spout directs liquid effluent into the pouch and away from the peristomal skin, which is especially important for an ileostomy's caustic output); the stoma has NO somatic sensory innervation (touching/cleaning it is painless) and is highly VASCULAR (minor bleeding/oozing when cleaned is normal and not a cause for alarm); mild EDEMA is expected for the first days-weeks and gradually subsides over ~6-8 weeks, so the stoma is RE-MEASURED at appliance changes and the barrier opening adjusted as it shrinks. ABNORMAL/EMERGENT findings to recognize + report: COLOR change to DUSKY, PURPLE, GRAY, BROWN, or BLACK = compromised blood supply (ISCHEMIA → NECROSIS) → NOTIFY THE SURGEON IMMEDIATELY (a surgical emergency); a pale stoma (anemia/poor perfusion); a dry, dull stoma (dehydration/poor viability). Other STOMAL/PERISTOMAL COMPLICATIONS: RETRACTION (stoma pulls below skin level → effluent leaks under the barrier → skin breakdown; managed with convex appliances + belt); PROLAPSE (stoma telescopes out, longer than expected → keep patient supine/cold compress may reduce edema, notify surgeon, watch for ischemia/incarceration); PARASTOMAL HERNIA (bulge around stoma); STENOSIS (narrowing of the stomal opening → thin/ribbon-like output, cramping, obstruction risk); MUCOCUTANEOUS SEPARATION (stoma edge separates from skin → wound care); and PERISTOMAL SKIN BREAKDOWN/IRRITANT DERMATITIS (the most common problem — from effluent contact due to poor fit/leakage; presents as red, weepy, painful skin; treat with proper refitting + barrier products + treat any candidal infection). NURSING: assess the stoma + peristomal skin at every appliance change + report abnormal color or new complications; document stoma color, size, height/budding, edema, effluent (amount/consistency/color), and skin condition.
labs.r: Healthy stoma = BEEFY-RED/PINK + moist + budded; painless to touch + minor oozing normal; edema subsides over weeks (re-measure). EMERGENT = dusky/purple/brown/black (ischemia/necrosis → notify surgeon STAT). Know retraction, prolapse, hernia, stenosis, mucocutaneous separation, and peristomal skin breakdown (most common). RN role: serial stoma + skin assessment + prompt reporting + documentation.

labs.v: Because an ileostomy bypasses the COLON (which normally reabsorbs ~1-1.5 L of water + sodium daily and forms stool), ileostomy effluent is LIQUID-to-PASTE, CONTINUOUS, and rich in WATER + SODIUM (and potassium) — making FLUID + ELECTROLYTE LOSS the dominant systemic risk. OUTPUT trajectory: immediately post-op the new ileostomy may be low, then as ileus resolves it produces liquid output that gradually thickens as the small bowel ADAPTS (increasing absorptive capacity) over the following weeks; expected MATURE output is roughly 500-1000 mL/day. HIGH-OUTPUT ILEOSTOMY = output exceeding ~1000-1200 mL/day (some define >1500) or persistently watery effluent → leads to DEHYDRATION, HYPONATREMIA, HYPOKALEMIA, hypomagnesemia, metabolic acidosis/alkalosis, and prerenal ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY if unaddressed; precipitants include early post-op period, partial obstruction, infection (e.g., C. difficile), abrupt steroid taper, certain foods/medications. RECOGNIZE DEHYDRATION: increased thirst, dry mouth, DARK/SCANT URINE (low urine output), dizziness/lightheadedness, ORTHOSTATIC vital changes, TACHYCARDIA, fatigue, muscle cramps, weight loss. MANAGEMENT: monitor STRICT INTAKE & OUTPUT (measure pouch output) + DAILY WEIGHT + electrolytes + renal function + signs of dehydration; ORAL REHYDRATION SOLUTIONS (balanced glucose-electrolyte solutions are absorbed better than plain water; plain water or hypotonic fluids alone can worsen sodium loss); IV fluid + electrolyte repletion if significant; ANTIMOTILITY/ANTIDIARRHEAL agents (loperamide, sometimes diphenoxylate-atropine; bile-acid binders or octreotide in refractory cases per provider) to slow transit + thicken output; DIETARY measures to thicken output (see nutrition section); replace SODIUM (liberal salt) and ensure adequate fluid; treat underlying causes. PATIENT TEACHING: how to measure/track output, the signs of dehydration and when to call, the importance of fluids + salt (and oral rehydration solution on high-output days), and that some output variability with diet/activity is normal.
labs.r: No colon → ileostomy effluent is liquid + high in water/sodium → DEHYDRATION + electrolyte loss is the dominant risk. Expected mature output ~500-1000 mL/day; HIGH-OUTPUT >~1000-1200 mL/day or watery → dehydration/hyponatremia/hypokalemia/AKI. Manage with strict I&O + daily weight + electrolytes + oral rehydration solution + IV repletion + loperamide + thickening diet + salt. RN role: monitor + replete + teach dehydration recognition.

labs.n: Pouching system + peristomal skin protection + appliance teaching
labs.v: POUCHING SYSTEM + PERISTOMAL SKIN PROTECTION (the proteolytic small-bowel effluent is highly damaging to skin, so a secure leak-free seal is essential): COMPONENTS — a SKIN BARRIER/WAFER (adhesive faceplate that protects peristomal skin) + a POUCH (collects effluent); ONE-PIECE systems (barrier + pouch integrated) vs TWO-PIECE systems (separate barrier + snap-on/adhesive-coupling pouch — allows changing the pouch without removing the barrier). FITTING — measure the stoma with a measuring guide and cut/mold the barrier opening to fit with only a SMALL CLEARANCE (about 1-2 mm / 1/8 inch) around the stoma: an opening TOO LARGE exposes peristomal skin to effluent (→ breakdown), and an opening TOO TIGHT can constrict/lacerate the stoma; because the new stoma SHRINKS over ~6-8 weeks, RE-MEASURE at each change and adjust. CONVEXITY + ACCESSORIES — a CONVEX barrier (and an ostomy BELT) helps a flush/retracted stoma protrude into the pouch for a better seal; barrier RINGS/PASTE/STRIPS fill irregularities/creases and protect skin; stoma POWDER (then sealant) treats denuded/weepy skin ('crusting' technique); skin prep/barrier films; treat peristomal candidiasis with antifungal powder. CARE PRACTICES — EMPTY the pouch when it is about 1/3 to 1/2 FULL (a heavy pouch pulls the seal loose and risks leakage; do not wait until full); CHANGE the wafer on a routine schedule (commonly every 3-7 days) AND IMMEDIATELY if there is leakage, or if the patient feels ITCHING/BURNING under the barrier (a key sign that effluent is reaching the skin); when changing, gently remove the barrier (adhesive remover/push skin from barrier rather than pulling), cleanse the peristomal skin with WATER (avoid oily soaps/lotions that impair adhesion), pat DRY (the barrier will not stick to wet skin), and apply a new barrier to clean dry intact skin; ODOR control via emptying, pouch deodorizers, and filters (most modern pouches are odor-proof when closed). TIMING tips — change the appliance when the stoma is least active (e.g., before eating/first thing in the morning) to minimize effluent during the change. The patient must demonstrate emptying and a full appliance change before discharge.
labs.r: Pouching = skin barrier/wafer + pouch (one- or two-piece). Cut the opening to fit with ~1-2 mm clearance (too big → skin breakdown; too tight → stoma injury); re-measure as the stoma shrinks. Convexity + belt for flush/retracted stomas; barrier rings/paste/powder protect skin. EMPTY at 1/3-1/2 full; change routinely (q3-7 days) + immediately for leak/itch/burn; clean with water + dry before applying. RN role: fit appliance + protect skin + teach + verify return demonstration.

labs.n: Diet, food-blockage prevention, and medication-absorption caveats
labs.v: DIETARY TEACHING for a new ileostomy: CHEW THOROUGHLY and eat slowly (the most important habit to prevent blockage); EAT REGULARLY (skipping meals can increase watery output/gas); introduce new foods ONE AT A TIME to learn individual tolerance; INITIALLY a LOW-FIBER/LOW-RESIDUE diet post-op, advancing as tolerated. FOODS THAT CAN CAUSE MECHANICAL BLOCKAGE (high-fiber/fibrous/hard-to-digest — eat in small amounts, chew very well, with plenty of fluid, or avoid early on): POPCORN, NUTS + SEEDS, RAW/STRINGY VEGETABLES (celery, coleslaw, cabbage), CORN, MUSHROOMS, DRIED FRUIT/RAISINS, fruit/vegetable SKINS + PEELS, COCONUT, and tough fibrous meats. GAS/ODOR-producing foods (manage per preference): eggs, fish, onions, garlic, asparagus, cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cabbage, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts), beans/legumes, carbonated beverages, beer; (some foods like parsley, yogurt, buttermilk may reduce odor). Foods that THICKEN loose output (helpful for high-output): applesauce, BANANAS, white RICE, PASTA, POTATOES (no skin), PEANUT BUTTER, MARSHMALLOWS, tapioca, pretzels, cheese. FLUIDS + SALT: drink adequate fluids (and ORAL REHYDRATION SOLUTION on high-output/hot days) + liberal salt to replace ileostomy losses (a low-salt/low-fluid approach is wrong for ileostomy patients). FOOD-BLOCKAGE RECOGNITION + RELIEF: a blockage presents as a SUDDEN DECREASE OR STOP in output WITH crampy abdominal pain, abdominal/stomal swelling, and possibly thin watery output around the blockage then nausea/vomiting; HOME measures (if no vomiting) = STOP solid food + switch to clear liquids, take a warm bath/shower (relaxes abdominal muscles), assume a KNEE-CHEST position, gently massage the abdomen/around the stoma, and increase fluids; REPORT/seek care if no output + pain persists, abdomen distends, or vomiting occurs (risk of obstruction/dehydration). MEDICATION-ABSORPTION CAVEAT: because transit is faster and the colon is absent, ENTERIC-COATED and EXTENDED/SUSTAINED/CONTROLLED-RELEASE oral medications may not fully dissolve/absorb (may be seen intact in the pouch) → prefer IMMEDIATE-RELEASE, liquid, chewable, or crushable formulations and consult PHARMACY; be alert that ileostomy patients are sensitive to fluid/electrolyte-altering drugs (diuretics) and that loperamide is used to control output. The patient should carry ostomy supplies + an emergency kit and know how to reach the WOC nurse.
labs.r: Diet: chew thoroughly, eat regularly, low-residue initially, introduce foods one at a time. Blockage-risk foods (eat sparingly + chew well): popcorn, nuts/seeds, raw/stringy veg, corn, mushrooms, dried fruit, skins/peels, coconut. Thickeners (applesauce/banana/rice/pasta/potato/peanut butter) help loose output. Ample fluid + SALT. Recognize/relieve blockage (clear liquids + warm bath + knee-chest + massage; report if vomiting/no output). Enteric-coated/extended-release meds may not absorb → use immediate-release/liquid + consult pharmacy. RN role: dietary + blockage + medication teaching.

labs.n: Psychosocial adjustment + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PSYCHOSOCIAL/BODY-IMAGE ADJUSTMENT: a new ostomy is a significant life change affecting body image, self-esteem, sexuality/intimacy, dating, clothing, work, exercise, and social activities; common feelings include grief, anxiety, embarrassment, fear of leakage/odor in public, and altered self-perception — these are NORMAL and should be acknowledged with empathy, not minimized. NURSING SUPPORT: provide a private, accepting environment; encourage the patient to look at and touch the stoma when ready (participation builds mastery + acceptance); involve the WOC NURSE (expert teaching + problem-solving + long-term resource); connect to PEER SUPPORT (United Ostomy Associations of America / UOAA, ostomy visitor programs) — meeting someone living well with an ostomy is powerfully reassuring; involve the partner/family per the patient's wishes; address SEXUALITY openly (ostomy does not preclude intimacy; tips for pouch management + reassurance); reassure that, once healed and skilled in self-care, people with ostomies return to a FULL ACTIVE LIFE (work, travel, swimming, exercise, relationships, pregnancy); screen for depression/anxiety + refer as needed; affirm that this surgery CURED her ulcerative colitis (no more flares, bleeding, urgency, steroids, or cancer-surveillance burden) — a reframe many patients find meaningful. PERTINENT NEGATIVES: stoma is BEEFY-RED + budded + viable (NO dusky/purple/black ischemia, no necrosis); peristomal skin INTACT (no irritant dermatitis/breakdown); no retraction/prolapse/mucocutaneous separation/stenosis; no obstruction/blockage (output present, no cramping-distension-vomiting); high-output episode on POD 3 was recognized + corrected (no severe dehydration/AKI); electrolytes corrected; afebrile + incision clean/dry/intact with no signs of surgical-site infection or anastomotic/intra-abdominal complication; hemodynamically stable; NKDA. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: PREOPERATIVE WOC stoma marking + education (strong predictor of good appliance fit + fewer complications); curative surgery for refractory UC with dysplasia (eliminates disease + cancer risk); young + otherwise healthy + motivated + good cognition; engaged supportive partner learning the care; comprehensive multidisciplinary team (colorectal surgery + WOC nurse + dietitian + pharmacy + social work + peer support); early recognition + correction of high-output dehydration; successful return demonstrations (independent pouch emptying + wafer change) before discharge; good insurance + supply access + arranged follow-up; psychosocial support addressing body image. EXPECTED trajectory: full recovery + independent ileostomy self-management + return to active life + resolution of UC symptoms; ongoing WOC follow-up + adaptation of output over weeks.
labs.r: Body-image/psychosocial adjustment is central: empathy + WOC nurse + peer support (UOAA) + partner involvement + sexuality discussion + reassurance of a full active life + the reframe that surgery cured her UC. Negatives: viable stoma, intact skin, no obstruction, corrected dehydration, no SSI. Protective: preop marking, motivated patient + supportive partner, multidisciplinary team, successful return demonstrations. RN role: psychosocial support + resources + confirm self-care competence.

nursesNote: POD 1 0800 — 26 y/o woman A. Delgado, post-op day 1 from TOTAL PROCTOCOLECTOMY WITH END ILEOSTOMY for an 8-year history of ULCERATIVE COLITIS that became medically refractory (steroid-dependent; failed 5-ASA + azathioprine + biologic; worsening bloody diarrhea + anemia + weight loss; recent severe flare with DYSPLASIA on surveillance biopsy). UC is limited to colon/rectum, so proctocolectomy is CURATIVE + removes cancer risk; counseled on options and chose a permanent end ileostomy. Stoma site MARKED PREOPERATIVELY by WOC nurse (flat, visible, within rectus, away from belt line/scars/folds/bony prominences); preop ostomy education begun. PMH: UC, iron-deficiency anemia; perioperative steroid stress-dose + taper managed. SOCIAL: graphic designer; supportive partner engaged in teaching; active (yoga/hiking); anxious about work/exercise/intimacy/clothing; nonsmoker; good insurance + strong support. POD 1: alert + comfortable on multimodal analgesia; abdomen soft, incision appropriately tender, no distension; STOMA RLQ = BEEFY-RED, moist, budded ~1.5 cm, mild expected edema; clear post-op pouch with scant bloody-tinged mucus (clear pouch early for visualization); peristomal skin intact; ambulated; IV fluids; voiding. VS HR 92, BP 112/70, RR 16, SpO2 98%, T 37.2. POD 2: bowel function returning — LIQUID effluent, ~600 mL/24 hr; stoma beefy-red/budded, edema decreasing; skin intact with well-fitted barrier; advancing clear → low-residue diet; IV fluids continued; I&O + daily weight + electrolytes monitored; began self-care teaching (pouch emptying) with WOC nurse. POD 3 1400: output rose to ~1300 mL/24 hr watery (HIGH-OUTPUT) with mild thirst + slightly dark urine + mild orthostasis (BP 104/64 lying → 96/58 standing, HR 104) = early dehydration; Na 134 + K 3.4 (low) → ORAL REHYDRATION SOLUTION started + IV fluid/electrolyte repletion + LOPERAMIDE per surgeon + output-thickening dietary measures taught + strict I&O/daily weight; reinforced dehydration-recognition teaching; peristomal skin intact. POD 4 0900: with rehydration + loperamide + thickening diet, output decreased to ~900 mL/24 hr thicker effluent; electrolytes corrected (Na 138, K 4.1); orthostasis resolved + urine clearing; stoma beefy-red, edema nearly resolved, REMEASURED for pouch sizing; patient emptied her own pouch + observed a wafer change; reviewing diet + medication teaching; anxious but engaging. POD 5 1000: stable; output ~700 mL/24 hr paste; healthy stoma + intact skin; patient performed a FULL pouch/wafer change with coaching (measured stoma, cut barrier with small ~1-2 mm clearance, applied without leakage) + partner taught; verbalized blockage + dehydration warning signs + dietary list + medication caveats (enteric-coated/extended-release may not absorb → immediate-release/liquid + pharmacy); met ostomy peer-support volunteer; body-image concerns addressed; discharge teaching nearly complete. POD 6: medically ready + tolerating regular low-residue diet + stable controlled output (~600-700 mL/24 hr) + stable electrolytes + healthy stoma + intact skin; DEMONSTRATED independent pouch empty + wafer change with teach-back; recognizes healthy vs abnormal stoma (report dusky/purple/black = ischemia STAT), dehydration, and blockage (clear liquids + warm bath + knee-chest + massage; report vomiting/no output); knows diet + fluid/SALT + medication caveats + when to call; outpatient WOC + colorectal surgery follow-up + ostomy supplies + UOAA peer-support resources arranged; emotional adjustment improving (reframe: surgery cured her UC); discharged home with partner.
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: surgical unit, post-op total proctocolectomy with end ileostomy
DIAGNOSIS: new end ileostomy s/p total proctocolectomy for medically refractory ulcerative colitis with dysplasia; ileostomy self-management education
CODE STATUS: full code; decisional adult
POST-OP CARE:
Vital signs per post-op protocol; monitor for hemodynamic instability + fever/SSI
Incision assessment; pain control with multimodal analgesia; early ambulation; DVT prophylaxis; incentive spirometry
Perioperative corticosteroid stress-dosing + taper (chronic steroid use); resume/adjust home meds
Advance diet clear liquids → low-residue as tolerated; monitor for return of bowel function
STOMA + PERISTOMAL SKIN:
Assess stoma every shift + at each appliance change — color (beefy-red/pink healthy; dusky/purple/brown/black = ischemia → NOTIFY SURGEON STAT), moisture, height/budding, edema; document
Assess + protect peristomal skin (intact vs irritant dermatitis/breakdown); treat with barrier products/crusting; antifungal for candidiasis
Clear pouch early for stoma visualization; re-measure stoma at changes as edema subsides (~6-8 wk)
WOC (wound-ostomy-continence) nurse consult — fitting + teaching + problem-solving
FLUID + ELECTROLYTES (high-output ileostomy risk):
Strict intake & output — MEASURE pouch output each shift; daily weight
Monitor electrolytes + renal function; assess for dehydration (thirst, dark/scant urine, orthostasis, tachycardia)
IV fluids until adequate oral intake; oral rehydration solution; replace sodium (liberal salt) + potassium
Antimotility agent (loperamide) per surgeon for high output; report output >1000-1200 mL/24 hr or watery effluent
POUCHING SYSTEM:
Skin barrier/wafer + pouch (one- or two-piece); cut opening to fit with ~1-2 mm clearance (not too large/too tight)
Convex barrier + belt if flush/retracted; barrier rings/paste/powder as needed
Empty pouch at 1/3-1/2 full; change wafer routinely (q3-7 days) + immediately for leak/itch/burn; clean with water + dry before applying
DIET + MEDICATION TEACHING:
Chew thoroughly + eat regularly; low-residue initially; introduce foods one at a time
Caution with blockage-risk foods (popcorn, nuts/seeds, raw/stringy vegetables, corn, mushrooms, dried fruit, skins/peels, coconut)
Output thickeners (applesauce, banana, rice, pasta, potato, peanut butter) for loose output; manage gas/odor foods per preference
Ample fluids + salt; oral rehydration solution on high-output days
Blockage recognition + home relief (clear liquids, warm bath, knee-chest position, abdominal massage; report vomiting/no output/distension)
Avoid enteric-coated/extended-release oral meds (may not absorb) — use immediate-release/liquid/chewable; pharmacy consult; caution with diuretics
PSYCHOSOCIAL + EDUCATION:
Encourage patient to view/touch/participate in stoma care when ready; empathetic body-image support; screen for depression/anxiety
Discuss sexuality/intimacy + return to work/exercise/activities; reassure full active life is achievable; reframe (UC cured)
Connect to peer support (United Ostomy Associations of America); involve partner per patient's wishes
Teaching with RETURN DEMONSTRATION + teach-back: empty pouch, full appliance change, stoma/skin assessment, dehydration + blockage recognition
MULTIDISCIPLINARY:
Colorectal surgery + WOC nurse + dietitian + pharmacy + social work (supplies/insurance/resources) + peer support
DISCHARGE PLANNING (when self-care competent + medically stable):
Independent (or support-person-assisted) pouch emptying + appliance change demonstrated
Ostomy supplies + emergency kit arranged; outpatient WOC + surgeon follow-up; supply vendor/insurance set up
Written instructions + WOC nurse contact + when to seek care (ischemic stoma color, no output with pain/vomiting/distension, signs of dehydration, peristomal skin breakdown, fever/SSI)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this new ileostomy?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: Stoma appearance — a healthy stoma is BEEFY-RED/PINK, moist, and budded; a DUSKY, PURPLE, BROWN, or BLACK stoma indicates ischemia/necrosis and must be reported to the surgeon immediately
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — stoma viability assessment; ischemia is an emergency. RN role: serial stoma assessment + prompt reporting.

opts.text: Ileostomy effluent is LIQUID and high in water/sodium because the colon is absent → dehydration + electrolyte loss is the dominant systemic risk; monitor strict I&O + daily weight + electrolytes + signs of dehydration
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fluid/electrolyte risk is central to ileostomy care. RN role: monitor output + hydration + electrolytes.

opts.text: High-output episode on POD 3 (~1300 mL watery) with thirst, dark urine, orthostasis, and low Na/K — early dehydration requiring oral rehydration solution, repletion, loperamide, and dietary thickening
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-output ileostomy with early dehydration needs prompt intervention. RN role: recognize + treat + teach.

opts.text: Peristomal skin protection — the proteolytic effluent excoriates skin; a properly fitted barrier with small clearance, empty at 1/3-1/2 full, and change immediately for leak/itch/burn
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — peristomal skin breakdown is the most common ostomy problem. RN role: proper fit + skin protection + teaching.

opts.text: Dietary + blockage teaching — chew thoroughly, low-residue initially, caution with blockage-risk foods (popcorn, nuts, raw/stringy vegetables, corn, mushrooms, dried fruit, skins); recognize/relieve a blockage
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diet + blockage prevention/recognition are essential self-management. RN role: dietary teaching + blockage education.

opts.text: Medication caveat — enteric-coated/extended-release oral medications may not fully absorb and can appear intact in the pouch; prefer immediate-release/liquid forms and consult pharmacy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — altered absorption affects medication safety/efficacy. RN role: medication review + pharmacy consult + teaching.

opts.text: Psychosocial/body-image adjustment — a new ostomy affects body image, sexuality, work, and activity; provide empathetic support, WOC nurse, and peer-support resources
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial adjustment is central to recovery + adherence. RN role: empathetic support + resources.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. Relevant for medications/adhesives. Not the priority cue.

opts.text: The stoma oozed a small amount of blood when cleaned — this requires immediate surgical exploration for hemorrhage
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The stoma is highly vascular; minor oozing/bleeding when cleaned is NORMAL and not an emergency. (Brisk/persistent bleeding would warrant evaluation, but minor oozing does not require surgical exploration.)

opts.text: Teach the patient to limit fluids and follow a low-salt diet to reduce the high ileostomy output
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS for an ileostomy. Patients LOSE water and sodium through the stoma and need AMPLE fluids + LIBERAL salt (and oral rehydration solution on high-output days). Fluid/salt restriction worsens dehydration + hyponatremia.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: Because the colon (water/sodium reabsorption + stool formation) is removed, ileostomy effluent is liquid + continuous + high in water/sodium → dehydration + electrolyte loss is the main systemic risk (vs a colostomy's more formed, lower-volume output)
opts.rat: CORRECT — anatomy explains the fluid/electrolyte risk + the ileostomy-vs-colostomy difference. Foundation for care.

opts.text: A healthy stoma is beefy-red/pink + moist + budded; painless (no sensory nerves) with normal minor oozing; edema subsides over ~6-8 weeks (re-measure); dusky/purple/black = ischemia/necrosis (emergency)
opts.rat: CORRECT — stoma assessment framework. Foundation for detecting compromise.

opts.text: High-output ileostomy (>~1000-1200 mL/day or watery) → dehydration/hyponatremia/hypokalemia/AKI → manage with oral rehydration solution, IV repletion, loperamide, output-thickening diet, salt, and strict I&O + daily weight
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-output management framework. Foundation for preventing dehydration complications.

opts.text: A barrier opening cut too large exposes skin to caustic effluent (breakdown), while one too tight constricts/injures the stoma; empty at 1/3-1/2 full; change for leak/itch/burn; protect skin with barrier products
opts.rat: CORRECT — pouching/skin-protection principles. Foundation for preventing the most common ostomy problem.

opts.text: Chewing thoroughly + a gradual low-residue diet + caution with fibrous/hard-to-digest foods prevents mechanical blockage; output-thickening foods help loose output; ample fluids + salt replace losses
opts.rat: CORRECT — dietary principles for blockage prevention + output management. Foundation for self-management.

opts.text: Enteric-coated/extended-release oral meds may not fully dissolve/absorb with faster transit + absent colon → use immediate-release/liquid forms + pharmacy consult
opts.rat: CORRECT — medication-absorption principle. Foundation for safe medication self-management.

opts.text: Because ulcerative colitis can recur, the patient must continue her UC biologic and immunosuppressants after surgery to prevent flares in the ileostomy
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Total proctocolectomy is CURATIVE for ulcerative colitis (UC is limited to the colon/rectum, which were removed); ongoing UC immunosuppression is not needed. (Crohn disease, by contrast, can recur elsewhere.)

opts.text: Since the stoma has no sensory nerve endings, peristomal skin condition is unimportant and routine skin assessment can be skipped
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The stoma is painless, but the surrounding PERISTOMAL SKIN is at high risk from caustic effluent and is the most common site of complications — it must be assessed + protected at every change.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: STOMA VIABILITY r/t early post-op ischemia risk — assess stoma color/perfusion each shift; beefy-red/pink is healthy, dusky/purple/black means ischemia/necrosis → notify surgeon immediately
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. A non-viable stoma is a surgical emergency; viability must be confirmed first. RN role: stoma assessment + prompt escalation.

opts.text: FLUID + ELECTROLYTE balance r/t high-output ileostomy — strict I&O + daily weight + electrolytes + dehydration signs; oral rehydration solution, IV repletion, loperamide, thickening diet, salt
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Dehydration/electrolyte loss is the dominant systemic danger for ileostomy patients. RN role: monitor + replete + teach.

opts.text: PERISTOMAL SKIN INTEGRITY + secure pouching r/t caustic effluent — proper barrier fit (small clearance), empty at 1/3-1/2 full, change for leak/itch/burn, protect skin
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Skin breakdown is the most common complication + undermines pouch security. RN role: fit + skin protection + teaching.

opts.text: SELF-MANAGEMENT EDUCATION r/t lifelong ostomy care — dietary + blockage + medication teaching with return demonstration of emptying + appliance change before discharge
opts.rat: #4 — Significant for safe discharge + long-term success. RN role: comprehensive teaching + verify competence.

opts.text: PSYCHOSOCIAL/BODY-IMAGE ADJUSTMENT r/t new ostomy — empathetic support, encourage participation, WOC nurse, peer support, sexuality/activity reassurance
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + for long-term coping/adherence. RN role: psychosocial support + resources.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this new ileostomy.

opts.text: Assess stoma color/moisture/budding + peristomal skin at each appliance change; report a dusky/purple/black stoma to the surgeon immediately
opts.rat: Core stoma surveillance; ischemia is an emergency. RN role: assess + report.

opts.text: Monitor strict I&O (measure pouch output) + daily weight + electrolytes; provide oral rehydration solution + IV repletion + liberal salt; give loperamide and thickening foods for high output
opts.rat: Manages the dominant fluid/electrolyte risk of an ileostomy. RN role: monitor + replete + teach.

opts.text: Fit a skin barrier cut to ~1-2 mm larger than the stoma, empty the pouch at 1/3-1/2 full, and change the wafer for leakage/itching/burning; protect skin with barrier products
opts.rat: Protects peristomal skin + maintains a secure seal. RN role: proper fit + skin care + teaching.

opts.text: Teach to chew thoroughly, eat low-residue initially, use caution with blockage-risk foods, and recognize/relieve a blockage (clear liquids, warm bath, knee-chest, massage; report vomiting/no output)
opts.rat: Prevents + addresses food blockage. RN role: dietary + blockage teaching.

opts.text: Review medications for enteric-coated/extended-release forms that may not absorb; consult pharmacy and use immediate-release/liquid alternatives
opts.rat: Ensures medication efficacy/safety with altered absorption. RN role: med review + pharmacy + teaching.

opts.text: Provide empathetic body-image support, encourage the patient to participate in stoma care when ready, and connect her to the WOC nurse + ostomy peer support
opts.rat: Supports psychosocial adjustment + mastery. RN role: support + resources.

opts.text: Have the patient demonstrate emptying the pouch and a full appliance change with teach-back before discharge
opts.rat: Confirms self-care competence for safe discharge. RN role: return demonstration + teach-back.

opts.text: Restrict the patient's fluids and recommend a low-salt diet to decrease the high ileostomy output
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — ileostomy patients lose water + sodium and need ample fluids + liberal salt; restriction causes dehydration + hyponatremia.

opts.text: Cut the barrier opening much larger than the stoma so it never touches the stoma, leaving a wide ring of exposed skin
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — an opening too large exposes peristomal skin to caustic effluent → breakdown. Cut to fit with only ~1-2 mm clearance.

opts.text: Encourage a high-fiber diet with popcorn, nuts, raw vegetables, and corn right away to keep output regular
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — these high-fiber/hard-to-digest foods risk mechanical blockage, especially early; diet should be low-residue initially with cautious gradual introduction + thorough chewing.

opts.text: Wait until the pouch is completely full before emptying to minimize how often it must be handled
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a full/heavy pouch pulls the seal loose and causes leakage; empty at about 1/3-1/2 full.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Action sequence for this new ileostomy
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the stoma (color/moisture/budding) + peristomal skin and confirm viability; report any dusky/purple/black color to the surgeon immediately
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Confirm the stoma is viable; ischemia is a surgical emergency. RN role: assessment + escalation.

opts.text: Monitor + measure pouch output, daily weight, and electrolytes; assess for dehydration and intervene (oral rehydration solution, IV repletion, loperamide, salt, thickening diet) for high output
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Manage the dominant fluid/electrolyte risk. RN role: monitor + replete.

opts.text: Ensure a secure, properly fitted pouching system and protect peristomal skin; empty at 1/3-1/2 full and change for any leak/itch/burn
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Protect skin + maintain containment. RN role: fit + skin protection.

opts.text: Teach dietary + blockage + medication self-management and have the patient return-demonstrate emptying + a full appliance change
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Build self-care competence for discharge. RN role: teaching + return demonstration.

opts.text: Provide psychosocial/body-image support, connect to the WOC nurse + peer support, and arrange supplies + outpatient follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/foundational. Support adjustment + continuity. RN role: psychosocial support + resources + discharge planning.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: POD 1-2: stoma beefy-red/budded with decreasing edema, peristomal skin intact, effluent established (~600 mL/24 hr), patient beginning self-care teaching (pouch emptying) with the WOC nurse
opts.rat: Healthy stoma + intact skin + appropriate output + engaged learning = expected normal early post-op course. Foundation for recovery.

opts.text: POD 3: output rose to ~1300 mL watery with thirst, dark urine, orthostasis, Na 134/K 3.4 → oral rehydration solution + IV repletion + loperamide + thickening diet started; teaching reinforced
opts.rat: Early high-output dehydration was DETECTED and treated promptly before serious harm — monitoring working as intended. With intervention the trajectory is corrected/improving.

opts.text: POD 4-5: output controlled (~700-900 mL paste), electrolytes corrected, orthostasis resolved, stoma healthy + skin intact, patient performs pouch empty + full wafer change with coaching
opts.rat: Output + electrolytes normalized + growing self-care competence. Foundation for safe discharge.

opts.text: POD 6: regular low-residue diet tolerated, stable controlled output + electrolytes, healthy stoma + intact skin, independent appliance change demonstrated with teach-back, follow-up + resources arranged → discharged
opts.rat: Discharge readiness met with demonstrated self-management. Excellent outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — on POD 1 the stoma turns dusky then purple-black with a dry dull surface; the nurse notifies the surgeon emergently for suspected stomal ischemia/necrosis requiring revision
opts.rat: ⚠️ Stomal ischemia/necrosis — a surgical emergency. Demonstrates why stoma color must be assessed + reported immediately. Requires urgent surgical evaluation/revision.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the barrier opening was cut far too large; by POD 3 the peristomal skin is red, weepy, denuded, and painful with the pouch repeatedly leaking
opts.rat: ⚠️ Peristomal irritant dermatitis from effluent contact due to poor fit. Demonstrates why the barrier must fit with small clearance + be changed for leak/itch/burn. Needs refitting + skin treatment.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — after eating popcorn and raw vegetables, the patient has a sudden stop in output with crampy pain, abdominal/stomal swelling, then nausea and vomiting (food blockage)
opts.rat: ⚠️ Food blockage/obstruction from high-residue foods. Demonstrates why blockage-risk foods are limited + chewed well; with vomiting/no output she needs evaluation (and home measures fail). Requires intervention.

opts.text: Outpatient week 4 — stoma matured/shrunk to its final size with output thickened and well-controlled, skin intact, patient independent and back at work, engaged with peer support and adjusting well
opts.rat: Expected adaptation over weeks + successful self-management + psychosocial adjustment. Standard ideal longer-term outcome.

opts.text: POD 1 the stoma shows mild edema and is budded/beefy-red; serial checks the same day before significant adaptation show the edema essentially the same — expected early post-op finding
opts.rat: Mild early post-op stomal edema is expected and will gradually subside over weeks; unchanged on same-day serial checks is normal (re-measure at appliance changes as it shrinks).

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
